text:
CT NECK WITH AND WITHOUT CONTRAST

CLINICAL INDICATION: 52-year-old female with 3-month history of left throat discomfort and odynophagia, now with a firm palpable left level II cervical lymph node approximately 2 cm on physical examination. Rule out malignancy. Referral from ENT.

COMPARISON: No prior imaging available for comparison.

TECHNIQUE: Axial CT images of the neck were obtained before and after the administration of approximately 100 mL of Omnipaque 350 intravenous contrast using a standard neck protocol (0.625 mm slice thickness, reconstructed to 3 mm axial, coronal, and sagittal reformats). Multiplanar reconstructions reviewed. No adverse reaction to contrast.

FINDINGS:

Primary site / oropharynx: There is subtle but definite asymmetric soft tissue fullness at the left base of tongue, best appreciated on the post-contrast sequences. The left base of tongue demonstrates mild asymmetric enhancement compared to the right side, without a discretely marginated mass. No invasion of the adjacent lingual tonsil or vallecula is identified. The right base of tongue, palatine tonsils, soft palate, and posterior pharyngeal wall are unremarkable. The hypopharynx and supraglottis appear grossly normal.

Cervical lymph nodes / left: There is an enlarged left level IIA cervical lymph node measuring approximately 2.1 x 1.8 cm in the axial plane. The node demonstrates heterogeneous internal enhancement with loss of the normal fatty hilum and an eccentric area of internal low attenuation, raising concern for early central necrosis. The nodal capsule appears intact without definite perinodal fat stranding. No adjacent vascular encasement identified. Additional subcentimeter nodes are present at the left level III region; these are not clearly abnormal by size criteria but are noted.

Cervical lymph nodes / right: No pathologically enlarged or morphologically abnormal cervical lymph nodes identified on the right. Bilateral level V, submandibular (level IB), and retropharyngeal regions appear unremarkable.

Parotid and submandibular glands: Symmetric in appearance. No focal lesion or ductal dilatation.

Thyroid: Symmetric, without focal nodule or mass. No substernal extension.

Vascular structures: Bilateral carotid arteries and jugular veins are patent. No vascular anomaly.

Airway: The larynx and trachea are patent. Vocal cords appear symmetric.

Bony structures / skull base: The cervical spine demonstrates normal alignment without destructive bony lesion. Skull base structures, including the clivus and condyles, appear intact.

Soft tissue / chest inlet: Partially visualized lung apices are clear. No superior mediastinal adenopathy seen on this limited evaluation.

Incidental: No significant incidental finding.

IMPRESSION:

1. Enlarged left level IIA cervical lymph node (2.1 x 1.8 cm) with internal heterogeneity and loss of fatty hilum, morphologically suspicious for metastatic nodal involvement.

2. Subtle asymmetric soft tissue fullness and enhancement at the left base of tongue without a discretely marginated mass on this examination. Given the associated nodal findings, this is concerning for an underlying primary lesion in this region, though the extent is difficult to characterize on CT alone. Further evaluation with direct visualization and/or MRI of the neck with contrast is recommended for lesion characterization and extent.

3. No contralateral cervical lymphadenopathy.

4. No bony destructive changes.

Overall, the constellation of findings is highly suspicious for a primary oropharyngeal malignancy with ipsilateral nodal involvement. Clinical and endoscopic correlation strongly advised. Tissue sampling recommended.
